guest: Jason Chicola, founder and CEO of Rev.com

business_ideas.idea: Rev.com: two-sided transcription marketplace connecting customers with freelance transcribers
Online math tutoring service (failed attempt, shut down after missing metrics)
VIP Kid-style online English teaching platform for remote educators

predictions.text: By the time I'm dead, a billion people will work remotely, up from much smaller number today
AI makes transcription markets bigger by enabling new use cases, not replacing jobs
Remote work technology will be huge factor in education industry transformation

dynamics: Sam guides a methodical, multi-layered interview where Jason tells his career arc (ODesk→Rev) and business philosophy. Jason provides detailed answers on marketplace strategy, naming decisions, workforce incentives, and AI's role. Conversational but strategic tone; Sam extracts both operational and visionary details, with notable digressions on Jason's mom's user-testing work and failed tutoring venture.
frameworks: Two-sided marketplace: staffing (hire person, roll dice) vs service (pay for guarantee)
Objective vs subjective work categories (transcription succeeds, design fails)
Creative constraints drive better decisions (pay for name later, limited budget)
Market expansion via affordability, not displacement (taxi market grew with Uber)
learnings: Working for free initially to demonstrate value is more effective than negotiating equity upfront when expectations are asymmetric
Most aspiring entrepreneurs benefit from high-growth venture-backed startup experience before founding
Choosing objective-quality categories (transcription) over subjective ones (design) enables standardization and workforce vetting
Guaranteeing service quality with vetting beats open marketplace hiring where customers must evaluate workers
AI expands markets by reducing costs and enabling new use cases, not eliminating jobs
Flexible work autonomy (pick your hours, no job applications) drives retention and satisfaction

banger_quotes.quote: By the time I'm dead, I think will be a billion people who work remotely
The people who care about quality need the human in the loop to make it accurate
Robots are coming for the jobs is not what we see

numbers_dropped: 50,000 people work on platform each month
40+ hours typical transcription turnaround pre-Rev, now couple hours
1 billion people projected working remotely by Jason's death
notable: Jason's mom story illustrates lifestyle impact of flexible remote work; detailed failure postmortem on their math tutoring experiment; extensive discussion of how they chose transcription because it's objective and quantifiable, avoiding subjective categories like design.
